content:
# Leviticus Chapter 27 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 75r–75v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_027_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_027_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 113–114 | **LXX Images:** `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_027_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_027_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**2** "Speak to the sons of Israel and say to them: When a man makes a special vow, the persons shall be yours by valuation to the LORD.

**3** If your valuation is of a male from twenty years old up to sixty years old, your valuation shall be fifty shekels of silver, according to the shekel of the sanctuary.

**4** If it is a female, your valuation shall be thirty shekels.

**5** If the person is from five years old up to twenty years old, your valuation shall be for a male twenty shekels, and for a female ten shekels.

**6** If the person is from a month old up to five years old, your valuation shall be for a male five shekels of silver, and for a female your valuation shall be three shekels of silver.

**7** And if the person is sixty years old or over, then your valuation for a male shall be fifteen shekels, and for a female ten shekels.

**8** But if he is too poor to pay your valuation, then he shall be made to stand before the priest, and the priest shall value him; according to what the one who vowed is able to afford, the priest shall value him.

**9** "If the vow is of an animal that may be offered as an offering to the LORD, all such that any man gives to the LORD is holy.

**10** He shall not exchange it or substitute for it, a good for a bad or a bad for a good; and if he does in any way substitute one animal for another, both it and the substitute shall be holy.

**11** And if it is any unclean animal that may not be offered as an offering to the LORD, then he shall present the animal before the priest,

**12** and the priest shall value it as either good or bad; as the priest values it, so it shall be.

**13** But if he wishes to redeem it, he shall add a fifth to your valuation.

**14** "And when a man dedicates his house as a holy thing to the LORD, the priest shall value it as either good or bad; as the priest values it, so it shall stand.

**15** And if the man who dedicates it wishes to redeem his house, he shall add a fifth to the valuation price, and it shall be his.

**16** "If a man dedicates to the LORD part of the land that is his possession, then your valuation shall be in proportion to its seed: a homer of barley seed at fifty shekels of silver.

**17** If he dedicates his field from the year of Jubilee, it shall stand at your full valuation.

**18** But if he dedicates his field after the Jubilee, then the priest shall calculate the price according to the years that remain until the year of Jubilee, and the valuation shall be reduced.

**19** And if he who dedicates the field wishes to redeem it, then he shall add a fifth to the valuation price, and it shall remain his.

**20** But if he does not wish to redeem the field, or if he has sold the field to another man, it shall not be redeemed anymore.

**21** But the field, when it is released in the Jubilee, shall be a holy thing to the LORD, like a field that has been devoted. The possession thereof shall be the priest's.

**22** "If a man dedicates to the LORD a field that he has bought, which is not a part of the field of his possession,

**23** then the priest shall calculate for him the amount of the valuation up to the year of Jubilee, and the man shall give the valuation on that day as a holy thing to the LORD.

**24** In the year of Jubilee the field shall return to him from whom it was bought, to whom the land belongs as a possession.

**25** Every valuation shall be according to the shekel of the sanctuary: twenty gerahs shall make a shekel.

**26** "But a firstborn animal, which as a firstborn belongs to the LORD, no man may dedicate; whether ox or sheep, it is the LORD's.

**27** And if it is an unclean animal, then he shall buy it back at your valuation and add a fifth to it; or if it is not redeemed, it shall be sold at your valuation.

**28** "But no devoted thing that a man devotes to the LORD, of anything that he has, whether man or animal, or of his possession in the land, shall be sold or redeemed; every devoted thing is most holy to the LORD.

**29** No devoted person who is to be devoted for destruction from mankind shall be ransomed; he shall surely be put to death.

**30** "Every tithe of the land, whether of the seed of the land or of the fruit of the trees, is the LORD's; it is holy to the LORD.

**31** If a man wishes to redeem some of his tithe, he shall add a fifth to it.

**32** And every tithe of herds and flocks, every tenth animal of all that pass under the herdsman's staff, shall be holy to the LORD.

**33** One shall not differentiate between good or bad, neither shall he make a substitute for it; and if he does make a substitute for it, both it and the substitute shall be holy; it shall not be redeemed."

**34** These are the commandments that the LORD commanded Moses for the sons of Israel on Mount Sinai.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **"When a man makes a special vow" (v. 2):** Hebrew כִּי יַפְלִא נֶדֶר. The verb יַפְלִא is from the root פָּלָא ("to be extraordinary, to be beyond ordinary, to be wonderful"). The form here (hiphil: "to make extraordinary/special") distinguishes this vow from the ordinary נֶדֶר ("vow"). LOGOS renders "makes a special vow" to preserve the nuance that this chapter concerns extraordinary dedications beyond ordinary worship. Some render simply "makes a vow" (ESV), losing the intensity of פָּלָא. The LXX renders εὐξηται εὐχήν ("shall vow a vow"), flattening the special character. The manifest notes correctly: the root pala points to extraordinary or surpassing obligation, not merely any vow.

2. **"The persons shall be yours by valuation" (v. 2):** Hebrew וְהָיוּ עֶרְכְּךָ נְפָשֹׁת לַיהוָה. The noun עֵרֶךְ ("valuation") is the chapter's central technical term — it refers to a fixed monetary equivalent assigned to a person or thing dedicated to God. LOGOS preserves "valuation" as a formal-equivalence rendering. The עֵרֶךְ is not a market price but a divinely ordained tariff by age and gender (vv. 3–8). "Yours" is addressed to Moses as the one setting the scales; the LXX reads τιμή ("price/value") rather than a specific valuation term.

3. **"Fifty shekels of silver, according to the shekel of the sanctuary" (v. 3):** Hebrew חֲמִשִּׁים שֶׁקֶל כֶּסֶף בְּשֶׁקֶל הַקֹּדֶשׁ. The phrase "shekel of the sanctuary" (שֶׁקֶל הַקֹּדֶשׁ) denotes the standard weight maintained at the sanctuary, as distinct from commercial weights that might vary regionally. It appears also at Exod 30:13, Num 3:47, and elsewhere in priestly legislation. LOGOS preserves the phrase in full. The LXX renders ἐν τῷ σίκλῳ τῷ ἁγίῳ ("in the holy shekel").

4. **"Valuation table" (vv. 3–7):** The chapter contains a graduated valuation table: males 20–60 years = 50 shekels; females 20–60 = 30 shekels; males 5–20 = 20 shekels; females 5–20 = 10 shekels; males 1 month–5 years = 5 shekels; females 1 month–5 years = 3 shekels; males 60+ = 15 shekels; females 60+ = 10 shekels. The scale reflects productive labor value in an agricultural economy, not intrinsic personal worth. LOGOS renders each tier precisely following the MT without adjustment. The LXX closely follows the MT in this table, with minor orthographic variation.

5. **"According to what the one who vowed is able to afford" (v. 8):** Hebrew עַל-פִּי אֲשֶׁר תַּשִּׂיג יַד הַנֹּדֵר. The idiom יַד תַּשִּׂיג ("the hand reaches") is a standard Hebrew expression for financial capacity — "what his hand can reach" = what he can afford. The same idiom appears in Lev 5:11; 14:21–22, 30–32 for graduated offerings for the poor. The priestly system thus provides an exemption from full valuation for the genuinely poor. LOGOS renders "is able to afford" (following NASB's tradition). The LXX renders ὅσον ἰσχύει ἡ χεὶρ τοῦ εὐξαμένου ("as much as the hand of the one who vowed is strong enough for").

6. **"He shall not exchange it or substitute for it" (v. 10):** Hebrew לֹא יַחֲלִיפֶנּוּ וְלֹא-יָמִיר אֹתוֹ. Two verbs are used: חָלַף ("to exchange, to change out") and מוּר (hiphil: "to substitute, to exchange"). The repetition with two near-synonyms intensifies the prohibition. The dedicated animal enters the sacred sphere permanently; no post-dedication exchange can dissolve its holy status. If substitution is attempted, both animals become holy (v. 10b). LOGOS renders both verbs: "exchange" and "substitute." The LXX uses ἀλλάξει and ἀλλάγματι ("exchange" and "something exchanged").

7. **"Dedicate his house as a holy thing" (v. 14):** Hebrew יַקְדִּישׁ אֶת-בֵּיתוֹ קֹדֶשׁ. The verb קָדַשׁ (hiphil: "to make holy, to consecrate, to set apart for God") with בֵּית ("house") indicates a voluntary act of transferring property into the sacred domain. Unlike the animal offerings (vv. 9–13), houses require priestly valuation before their status is fixed. The valuation determines the redemption price if the owner later wishes to reclaim the house. LOGOS renders "dedicates his house as a holy thing" preserving the technical language. The LXX renders ἁγιάσῃ τὴν οἰκίαν αὐτοῦ ἁγίαν ("sanctifies his house as holy").

8. **"Homer of barley seed at fifty shekels of silver" (v. 16):** Hebrew זֶרַע חֹמֶר שְׂעֹרִים בַּחֲמִשִּׁים שֶׁקֶל כָּסֶף. The homer (חֹמֶר) was a donkey-load, approximately 220–230 liters. Barley seed capacity was used to measure land area: one homer of seed could sow a field of approximately 10 acres. The valuation at 50 shekels per homer of barley seeding capacity establishes a standard land price. The use of seed-capacity rather than area measurement reflects ancient Near Eastern practice. LOGOS preserves "homer of barley seed" as a formal unit. The LXX renders σπόρου κόρου κριθῶν ("seed of a kor of barley") — a kor being equivalent to a homer in Greek usage.

9. **"From the year of Jubilee" / "after the Jubilee" (vv. 17–18):** Hebrew מִשְּׁנַת הַיֹּבֵל / אַחַר הַיֹּבֵל. The timing of the dedication relative to the Jubilee cycle determines its value: a field dedicated immediately after a Jubilee has its full 49-year value; one dedicated later has proportionally reduced value since fewer productive years remain before the Jubilee resets ownership. The priest calculates the prorated price (v. 18). This is sophisticated sacred-economic reasoning. The LXX reads ἀπὸ τοῦ ἔτους τῆς ἀφέσεως / μετὰ τὴν ἄφεσιν ("from the year of release / after the release").

10. **"A field that has been devoted" (v. 21):** Hebrew כִּשְׂדֵה הַחֵרֶם. The comparison to a חֵרֶם-field (a devoted/banned field) signals that unredeemed dedicated land permanently transfers to priestly ownership. The cherem (חֵרֶם) is the most severe category of dedication — irrevocable, belonging absolutely to God/the sanctuary. A dedicated field that reaches the Jubilee unredeemed is functionally treated as a cherem-field: it does not revert to the original owner but passes permanently to the priests. LOGOS renders "a field that has been devoted" rather than "a devoted field" to signal the connection to the cherem legislation of vv. 28–29. The LXX renders γῆ ἡ ἀφωρισμένη ("land that has been set apart").

11. **"A devoted thing" / "cherem" (vv. 28–29):** Hebrew חֵרֶם. The root חרם denotes the most severe form of religious dedication: total, irrevocable transfer to God. What is cherem cannot be sold, redeemed, or reclaimed (v. 28). A person placed under cherem for destruction (v. 29 — in the context of war-criminals or those utterly given over to divine judgment) cannot be ransomed. The noun is deliberately kept distinct from the ordinary vow valuations of vv. 1–27. LOGOS renders "devoted thing" (following ESV/NASB tradition) rather than transliterating cherem, preserving the formal-equivalence practice of translating technical terms where an English equivalent exists. The LXX renders ἀνάθεμα ("anathema"), a Greek term that entered Christian vocabulary from exactly this passage. Note: the Vaticanus is legible on both pages and shows ἀνάθεμα consistently at vv. 28–29.

12. **"Every devoted thing is most holy to the LORD" (v. 28b):** Hebrew קֹדֶשׁ-קָדָשִׁים הוּא לַיהוָה. The superlative construction קֹדֶשׁ הַקֳּדָשִׁים ("holy of holies" / "most holy") is the highest degree of holiness in the priestly gradient: it is applied to the Ark, the altar, and the high-priestly offerings. Here it is applied to anything placed under cherem — elevating devoted things to the same sacred category as the innermost sanctuary. LOGOS renders "most holy to the LORD" (NASB tradition). The LXX renders ἅγιον ἁγίων ἐστὶν τῷ κυρίῳ ("it is holy of holies to the LORD").

13. **"No devoted person who is to be devoted for destruction from mankind shall be ransomed; he shall surely be put to death" (v. 29):** Hebrew כָּל-חֵרֶם אֲשֶׁר יָחֳרַם מִן-הָאָדָם לֹא יִפָּדֶה מוֹת יוּמָת. This is one of the most severe verses in the Pentateuch. The חֵרֶם applied to persons (as in Num 21:2; Deut 20:17; 1 Sam 15:3) designated total destruction in holy war contexts. The phrase מוֹת יוּמָת ("he shall surely be put to death") uses the intensified infinitive absolute. LOGOS renders the verse literally without softening. The LXX renders πᾶν ὃ ἐὰν ἀνατεθῇ ἀπὸ τῶν ἀνθρώπων οὐ λυτρωθήσεται ἀλλὰ θανάτῳ θανατωθήσεται ("everything devoted from mankind shall not be ransomed but shall be put to death by death").

14. **"Tithe" / ma'aser (vv. 30–33):** Hebrew מַעֲשֵׂר, from עָשַׂר ("ten"). The tithe is a tenth of agricultural produce (v. 30: grain and fruit) and a tenth of the herd or flock (v. 32). LOGOS renders "tithe" throughout as the established English equivalent. Two categories appear: (1) agricultural tithe of seed and fruit (v. 30), which is holy to the LORD; (2) animal tithe (vv. 32–33), determined by the herdsman's staff as animals pass beneath it — every tenth animal, without selection of good or bad, is taken. The LXX renders δεκάτη ("tenth") consistently.

15. **"Pass under the herdsman's staff" (v. 32):** Hebrew כֹּל אֲשֶׁר-יַעֲבֹר תַּחַת הַשָּׁבֶט. The image is of a staff held low to the ground at the entrance to the pen, as the animals file out one by one; the herdsman counts, and every tenth is consecrated. The staff (שֵׁבֶט) functions here as a counting instrument. The word שֵׁבֶט can also mean "tribe" or "scepter," but here is clearly "staff." LOGOS renders "pass under the herdsman's staff" following NASB/ESV tradition. The LXX renders ὅσα ἐὰν ἔλθῃ ὑπὸ τὴν ῥάβδον ("whatever passes under the rod").

16. **"One shall not differentiate between good or bad" (v. 33):** Hebrew לֹא-יְבַקֵּר בֵּין-טוֹב לָרַע. The verb בִּקֵּר in this context means to "examine, distinguish, separate out." The tithe animal is selected by order of passage, not quality. The herdsman cannot examine the flock and pick the inferior animals for the tithe while keeping the best. The uncalculated nature of the tithe — pure lot — is itself an expression of consecration without calculation. LOGOS renders "shall not differentiate." The LXX renders οὐ διαστελεῖ καλὸν ἀπὸ φαύλου ("shall not distinguish good from bad").

17. **"These are the commandments that the LORD commanded Moses for the sons of Israel on Mount Sinai" (v. 34):** Hebrew אֵלֶּה הַמִּצְוֹת אֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֶת-מֹשֶׁה אֶל-בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל בְּהַר סִינָי. This is the closing colophon of the entire book of Leviticus. The word מִצְוֹת ("commandments") is plural, encompassing all the legislation given in the book. The phrase בְּהַר סִינַי ("on Mount Sinai") appears uniquely to frame Leviticus at its opening (25:1) and now at its close — a literary inclusio tying the entire Sinai-discourse of Leviticus (whether spoken at the Tent of Meeting or on the mountain) to the covenant moment at Sinai. The same colophon form appears at the close of Numbers (Num 36:13) and in slight variation at Lev 7:37–38; 11:46–47; and 26:46. LOGOS renders the verse with formal precision. The LXX renders αὗται αἱ ἐντολαί ἃς ἐνετείλατο κύριος τῷ Μωυσῇ πρὸς τοὺς υἱοὺς Ισραηλ ἐν τῷ ὄρει Σινα ("these are the commandments which the LORD commanded Moses for the sons of Israel on Mount Sinai") — closely following the MT with standard LXX equivalents.

18. **Colophon and book-end theology:** The book of Leviticus does not conclude with a narrative event but with a legal declaration that all these commandments come from the LORD via Moses at Sinai. The effect is to position the entire book as divine speech, not Mosaic innovation. Chapter 27 functions as an appendix on vows — it comes after the climactic blessings and curses of chapter 26 (which is the natural theological conclusion of Leviticus) and may represent a secondary addition. Yet the colophon of v. 34 ties the appendix into the authoritative framework of the whole. In the canonical shape of Leviticus, the book ends where it began: at Sinai, with the voice of the LORD to Moses.

19. **LXX Significant Variants (Vaticanus, pp. 113–114):**
    - V. 2: LXX omits the specific phrase "special vow" nuance; εὐχήν is simply "a vow" without the extraordinary force of יַפְלִא. The Hebrew's emphasis on the surpassing character of the dedication is lost.
    - V. 10: LXX uses ἀλλάξει and ἀλλάγματι in parallel, slightly different from MT's two distinct verbs חָלַף / מוּר, but the effect is equivalent.
    - V. 16: LXX reads κόρου κριθῶν ("a kor of barley") for MT's חֹמֶר שְׂעֹרִים ("a homer of barley") — a kor and homer are equivalent volume measures, so this is a translation equivalence rather than a textual variant.
    - V. 21: LXX renders "set-apart land" (γῆ ἡ ἀφωρισμένη) rather than explicitly linking to the cherem concept of vv. 28–29. The MT's internal cross-reference via the word חֵרֶם connecting vv. 21 and 28 is thus weaker in the LXX.
    - V. 29: LXX preserves the severity of מוֹת יוּמָת with θανάτῳ θανατωθήσεται — the cognate accusative construction "shall be put to death by death" — mirroring the MT's infinitive absolute intensification precisely.
    - V. 34: LXX follows MT closely. Vaticanus page 114 shows the colophon clearly legible as the final line, confirming the textual tradition is stable. No significant variants in the closing verse.

20. **Formal structure of Leviticus 27:** The chapter falls into five legal sections:
    - §1 (vv. 1–8): Valuation of persons dedicated by extraordinary vow.
    - §2 (vv. 9–13): Valuation of dedicated animals (clean and unclean).
    - §3 (vv. 14–25): Valuation of dedicated houses and land (ancestral and purchased).
    - §4 (vv. 26–29): Exceptions — firstborn animals (already God's), devoted things (cherem, irredeemable), devoted persons (cherem, irredeemable).
    - §5 (vv. 30–33): The tithe as a separate category of holy obligation.
    - Colophon (v. 34): Closing formula for the entire book.
    The unifying theological logic is that everything placed into the holy sphere (whether voluntarily or by obligation) belongs to the LORD and operates by its own fixed rules — not by market logic but by divine valuation.
